children.text: ) refers to the linguistic style artists have curated over the years to perform reggaetón that draws inspiration from the syllabic syncopation of dancehall DJs' ragga-moofin flip-tongue style of rap and the staccato, double-time, end-rhyme orientation of mainstream hip hop. It is an artistic derivative of working-class dialects of Puerto Rican Spanish spoken in the

(housing projects) of San Juan, the surrounding neighborhoods, and the New York diaspora that has resisted erasure and modification amidst the genre's globalization. That is, while other forms of Spanish-language popular music have been subjected to stylistic editing to approximate a neutralized, transnational linguistic standard, reggaetón has successfully flipped the script to prioritize the use of local vernacular features that, at times, are stigmatized as deviations from standard linguistic practice.

This Atlas is a space wherein reggaetón researchers and linguists can share their research about the linguistic enactment and perception of reggaetón itself. Below is an outline for how each entry is structured.
Each of the following entries includes two major sections:

This section provides an introduction to the linguistic variable in a technical sense, balancing accessibility for both linguists and non-linguists alike. This includes a review of the feature's origins, linguistic structure, social stratification, and stylistic associations. Then, relevant findings from research on the dispersion, use, and perception of the feature in Puerto Rico are presented to situate the feature's use in contexts outside reggaetón.

This section guides readers through sonic examples of actual reggaetón productions to exemplify the findings of research specifically on the feature's use in reggaetón. Lyrical transcriptions with basic linguistic annotations are provided with each example for accessibility.
This offers a bibliography for those who would like a deeper dive into the literature being summarized in the Atlas.
